free_text: Slow neutron velocity spectrometer studies. I. Cd, Ag,
Sb, Ir, Mn
The Columbia Neutron Velocity
Spectrometer.
Two parallel independent BF3 proportional
counter detectors. The new counters have cathodes 10
cm in length, about 5 cm diameter, and are filled to
50-cm pressure with purified BF3 enriched in B10
content for greater efficiency.
In normal operation time of flight measurements
of less than 640 microsec (at 6.2 meters) are
obtained by using a 1280-microsec period for the
complete operation cycle and using thick Cd in the
beam to remove all neutrons having times of flight
greater than about 800 microsec. This prevents
overlap from previous cycles. In the new spectrometer
system 16 detection intervals are measured
simultaneously.
Compiler's comment: Energy scale is confusing, so
plots were digitized vs. neutron time of flight in
microsec/meter and converted to energy using the
relation E = (72.3/t) **2 eV which gives the neutron
energy for a timing t, microsec/meter from. Formula
was taken Phys. Rev. 71, 165 (1947) and verified using
the method of visual inspection. Transmission data
are more reliable, while cross section scale is less
obvious.
Translated from SCISRS.
Converted to reaction formalism
BP: Added method, detector, error-
analysis, digitized data sets and doi. Updated to new
date formats, lower case. Corrections according last
EXFOR rules and Dict.
16.24 g/cm2 sample of pure electrolytic manganese.
The cross section, sigma, is related to the
transmission, T, by the relation T=exp(-n sigma)
where n=atoms/cm2 of the element studied (if only the
element is present).
Experimental uncertainties.
Figs. 9-10, page 76.
BP: Replaced BNL-325 curve data with
digitized data, added sample information.
43.8 mg/cm2 sample. The samples used for the cadmium
transmission measurements were cut from sheets rolled
to 0.010" and 0.022" thicknesses. A spectrographic
analysis of the cadmium showed less than 0.05 percent
of Pb and Tl; less than 0.005 percent of Al, Ag, and
Cu; traces of Ca, Cr, Fe, Sn.
The statistical uncertainties of the points have not
been indicated as their magnitude is often equal in
size to the circles used to indicate the experimental
points.
Figs. 3-5, pages 72-73.
BP: Replaced BNL-325 curve data with
digitized data, added sample information.
6.84 g/cm2 sample. The samples used for the
transmission measurements on antimony were cast disks
of the metal. A spectrographic analysis of the
antimony showed less than 0.05 percent of Ca, Ag, and
Si; less than 0.005 percent of Mg, Ni, Cu, and Ge;
traces of Co, Pb, and As. For all other metals the
prominent lines were not apparent therefore the
impurity was probably less than 0.05 percent for arc
insensitive materials or less than 10**-5 percent for
arc sensitive materials.
The cross section, sigma, is related to the
transmission, T, by the relation T=exp(-n sigma)
where n=atoms/cm2 of the element studied (if only the
element is present).
Experimental uncertainties.
Fig. 8, page 75.

7.84 g/cm2 sample. The samples used for the
transmission measurements were of metallic silver. A
spectrographic analysis of the silver showed less
than 0.05 percent of Cu, Pb; less than 0.005 percent
of Mg, Mn, Fe, Tl, and Bi. For all the other metals
the prominent lines were not apparent, therefore the
impurity was probably less than 0.05 percent for arc
insensitive materials or less than 10**-5 percent for
arc sensitive materials.
